text: to have found it. The contract theories of the seventeenth century aimed to organize society according to the parameters of rational thought established in philosophy and science. What should also be considered, however, is the kind of human self that was institutionalized alongside this process. As was custom among the contract theorists, Hobbes would typically devote the first section of his reasoning to a discussion of man and human nature. The geometrical grid that Hobbes pursued required basic definitions. This first definitional part, then, directed the political analysis and its programmatic results. The implicit idea, still dominant in much political theory and ideology, is that once we know and can declare human nature as, for example, either individualistic or collective, peaceful or warlike, we also know how to govern. This construct is inherently blind to another possibility: that ‘human nature’ is likewise a product of rule and power. 11 The epistemological status of this ‘first principle’ in Hobbes’ philosophy is not our issue here; but it should of course be stressed that Hobbes did not claim to have found absolute truth as did Descartes. He did claim, however, to have singled out the cause-effect relationships as they functioned in the empirical world, and he also claimed to have a good idea (a ‘first principle’) for improving them. Liminality and the Modern 128 The immediate parallels with Descartes are clear enough in Hobbes’ main work, Elementa Philosophiae, which was composed of the three sections, De Corpore, De Homine and De Cive (although the three pieces were not published together under this title until 1668). First of all, Hobbes started out his entire work by repeating Descartes’ radical doubt about the existence of the world: the world and its magnitudes, motions, sounds and colours appear as if they are external objects even if they are in fact ‘phantasms internally happening’ (Hobbes from De Corpore, translated and quoted by Tuck 1998: xv). Hobbes argued that there is no way in which we can really know anything about the external world: we are simply imagining an external world through our ‘internal phantasms’; on this point Hobbes may have been under the direct influence of Descartes (see Tuck 2002). But if we cannot know about the world, then we can direct our investigation to that ‘inner reality’ which is the self, or human nature. It was here that Hobbes substantially disagreed with Descartes concerning the nature of the human – as he disagreed with Descartes’ proof of the existence of God and the possibility that this ‘proof’ could inject veracity into our sense-impressions. At the same time, the two arguments are also very similar: we cannot know about the external world, so let us turn the gaze inwards. Establish from within an unquestionable truth, and construct a system of order that flows from there towards the world. The projects are readily comparable as acts of world-making and forms of order-bestowing. Hobbes’ First Principles What, then was Hobbes’ axiom? This he stated very clearly in the first edition of De Cive, published in Paris in 1642, when Hobbes had already fled to Paris, scared by the prospects of civil war. Presenting the essence of his work in the Epistle dedicatory to De Cive, Hobbes claimed that ‘I obtained two absolutely certain postulates of human nature’, namely ‘human greed’ and ‘natural reason, by which each man strives to avoid violent death as the supreme evil in nature’ (Hobbes in Tuck and Silverthorne 1998: 6). Hobbes’ first principle, his ‘uncaused cause’, was the principle of self- preservation: the right for each man to protect his life and limbs as far as he can. It was important for Hobbes to stress that this foundation was a natural right and therefore just (De Cive, 1.7, in ibid.: 27). To Hobbes, this political principle of self-preservation could likewise function as a foundation for ethics. In De Cive Hobbes launched yet another attack on the ancients, which was here directed not only against their ‘naïve realism’ but specifically against the Aristotelian idea that man is an animal ‘born fit for society’. No, says Hobbes, we are not born to live together in peace and harmony. If people seek each other’s company they do it to maximize personal honour, to gain a personal advantage, or to enjoy their own glory. The logical conclusion followed that ‘the origin of large and lasting societies lay not in mutual human benevolence but in men’s mutual fear’ (Hobbes in ibid.: 24). Hobbes, of course, did insist on linking political order to more noble Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 129 civic virtues, such as gratitude (see Dietz 1990b), but such virtues could only be re-injected while based upon a recognition of the true human nature. Hobbes famously restates his notion of the self in his most well-known work, Leviathan, finished in the aftermath of the Treaty of Westphalia, and ‘occasioned by the disorders of the present time’, as Hobbes says in his conclusion, with a clear reference to the English civil war (Hobbes 1968: 728). 12 The human person from which Hobbes built his model is a passionate individual, isolated in its origins, and motivated primarily by its passions for power: ‘I put for a generall inclination of all mankind, a perpetuall and restlesse desire of Power after power, that ceaseth onely in death’ (Leviathan, Part I, Chapter XI; here quoted from Hobbes 1968: 161). The Hobbesian person possessed an essentially unordered, anarchic self. Therefore, argued Hobbes, the common good of society cannot be established on the basis of this self. Here, of course, Hobbes was deeply anti-Cartesian. 13 However, he nonetheless established a different kind of first principle within the human self. What may eventually bring individuals to reason is fear: fear of death, fear of annihilation. This mutual fear, for Hobbes, was the only ‘positive’ passion with which society could be held together. In so doing, Hobbes took a dangerous type of human being for granted as the normal human type, and devised the Leviathan
